message:
fix(pane-web): yield pane touch capture to multi-touch gestures

bd-27s9r

- add native touch gesture cancellation to the pane pointer adapter

- expose touch-specific wasm runner dispatch

- track active touch pointers in the showcase host before suppressing scroll and pinch

update on `bd-16jx8`:

- Landed a semantics upgrade that makes pane divider interaction feel closer to GUI behavior at the input layer:
  - axis-locked drag thresholding and delta projection in `PaneDragResizeMachine`
  - damped-spring motion filtering in web pointer capture (`ftui-web`) on the primary split axis

- Added regression tests for spring settle/overshoot and axis-lock behavior.
- Validated this slice with targeted `rch exec -- cargo check/clippy/test` commands (all passing for `ftui-layout` + `ftui-web` scoped checks used here).

Architecture note from code investigation: pane semantic transitions are still largely adapter-level in current runtime/demo wiring; full model-level push/resist/collide behavior will require a follow-on bridge from transitions into live `PaneTree` ratio mutation paths.

 {"id":"bd-16jx8.1","title":"task(pane-ux): advanced organic pane interactions (edge resize, inertial move, docking, grouping, modes, timeline)","description":"Implement the full advanced pane UX stack in shared pane core + web adapter.

Scope:
1) Any-edge / any-corner organic resize with constraint-aware neighbor reflow.
2) Flick-to-throw pane motion with momentum + damped settle.
3) Magnetic docking fields with live ghost preview targets.
4) Group move/resize for multi-pane selection preserving internal ratios.
5) Pressure-sensitive snap behavior from drag speed + direction changes.
6) Adaptive layout intelligence modes (Focus/Compare/Monitor/Compact).
7) Persistent interaction timeline for undo/redo and deterministic replay.

Validation:
- Add unit tests for all new policy/operation/timeline paths.
- Extend web pointer-capture tests for velocity/snap/docking semantics.
- Keep operation logs deterministic and host-agnostic.

 {"id":"bd-16k","title":"Unicode width corpus testing with WTF-8","description":"TESTING - UNICODE WIDTH CORPUS

## Background
Unicode width calculation is notoriously complex with edge cases around emoji, CJK, combining characters, and ZWJ sequences. A comprehensive test corpus is essential for correctness.

## Technical Requirements
- Test corpus categories:
  - Basic ASCII (width 1)
  - CJK Unified Ideographs (width 2)
  - Fullwidth ASCII variants (width 2)
  - Halfwidth Katakana (width 1)
  - Emoji with modifiers (width varies)
  - ZWJ sequences (flag emoji, family emoji)
  - Combining characters (width 0)
  - Control characters (width 0)
  - Ambiguous width characters (context-dependent)
- WTF-8 handling for unpaired surrogates
- Terminal-specific width (vs Unicode Standard)
- Test against unicode-width crate results

## Known Edge Cases
- 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 (family emoji ZWJ) - varies by terminal
- 🏳️‍🌈 (flag ZWJ) - often renders as 2 cells
- Regional indicators (flag pairs)
- Variation selectors (VS15/VS16)

## Design Rationale
Correctness in width calculation directly impacts rendering quality. The corpus captures real-world edge cases that simple tests miss.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Corpus with 1000+ test cases
- [ ] Categorized by width type
- [ ] Known terminal-specific behaviors documented
- [ ] Property tests for grapheme iteration
- [ ] Fuzz testing for malformed input
- [ ] Benchmark for width calculation performance

## Location: ftui-text crate tests/","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:20:38.382705649Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T18:45:37.439264503Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T18:45:37.439245598Z","close_reason":"Completed: Created comprehensive Unicode width corpus with 100+ explicit test cases across 15 categories, property tests with proptest for grapheme iteration, and benchmarks for performance testing","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-16k","depends_on_id":"bd-2uk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T16:31:30Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-16k","depends_on_id":"bd-e211","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T16:31:30Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-16pal","title":"task(graveyard-§6.9): Continuous-Time FRP for animations (below threshold, Score 1.5)","description":"Continuous-Time Functional Reactive Programming (CT-FRP) for animation system. Below the adoption threshold with Score 1.5 -- documented for completeness but not recommended for near-term implementation.

## Graveyard Entry

Section 6.9 -- Continuous-Time FRP
Priority: 4 (Below threshold)
EV Score: 1.5

## Background and Math Foundations

CT-FRP (Elliott and Hudak 1997, Functional Reactive Animation) models time-varying values as continuous functions of time, with discrete events as point occurrences. Unlike discrete FRP (which FrankenTUI already uses via Elm architecture), CT-FRP represents animations as mathematical functions:

- Behavior A = Time -> A (continuous signal)
- Event A = [(Time, A)] (discrete occurrences)
- Composition: lift2(f, b1, b2) = t -> f(b1(t), b2(t))

For animations: spring(target, stiffness, damping) is a Behavior of Position defined by the ODE:
  x'' + 2*damping*x' + stiffness*x = stiffness*target

FrankenTUI already has damped spring physics. CT-FRP would provide a UNIFIED framework for ALL time-varying values (springs, easing, transitions, blinks), replacing ad-hoc per-animation-type implementations.

## Why Below Threshold

- FrankenTUI already has damped spring physics (the highest-value animation primitive)
- CT-FRP adds significant API complexity for marginal benefit
- The continuous-time model conflicts with discrete frame rendering
- Risk of abstraction astronomy -- elegant math that complicates implementation

## Recommendation

Defer indefinitely. If animation complexity grows beyond 5+ distinct animation types, reconsider.

## Structured Logging (if ever implemented)

All animation tests must emit structured JSONL evidence:
- op: ct_frp_sample, behavior_type, time_ns, value, expected_value, error
- op: ct_frp_compose, composition_depth, eval_time_ns, allocation_count
- op: ct_frp_benchmark, animation_type, ct_frp_time_ns, adhoc_time_ns, quality_diff

## Acceptance Criteria (if ever implemented)

1. Behavior and Event types with continuous-time semantics
2. Spring, easing, and transition animations expressed in CT-FRP
3. Efficient sampling at frame boundaries
4. No regression in animation quality or performance
5. API complexity justified by reduction in animation code volume
6. All tests emit structured JSONL logs for evidence-ledger integration","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:25.501248289Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:28:21.021155752Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","animation","below-threshold","continuous-time","frp","graveyard"]}

 {"id":"bd-27kx","title":"Text Effects: Organic Breathing Pulse","description":"## Background
Simple sine wave pulsing looks mechanical. Organic breathing has asymmetric timing (quick inhale, slow exhale) and per-character variation for a living, natural feel.

## Implementation

Add TextEffect variant:
```rust
TextEffect::OrganicPulse {
    speed: f64,            // Breaths per second (0.5 = one breath every 2s)
    min_brightness: f64,   // 0.0-1.0, lowest brightness
    asymmetry: f64,        // 0.0=symmetric, 1.0=very asymmetric (quick in, slow out)
    phase_variation: f64,  // 0.0-1.0, how much chars vary in phase
    seed: u64,             // Deterministic randomness for phase offsets
}
```

### Asymmetric Breathing Curve:
```rust
fn breathing_curve(t: f64, asymmetry: f64) -> f64 {
    // t is 0-1 through one breath cycle
    // asymmetry controls rise/fall ratio
    let rise_duration = 0.3 - asymmetry * 0.2;  // 10-30% of cycle for inhale
    
    if t < rise_duration {
        // Quick rise (inhale) - use quadratic ease-out
        let local_t = t / rise_duration;
        1.0 - (1.0 - local_t) * (1.0 - local_t)  // Ease-out
    } else {
        // Slow fall (exhale) - use quadratic ease-in-out
        let local_t = (t - rise_duration) / (1.0 - rise_duration);
        if local_t < 0.5 {
            1.0 - 2.0 * local_t * local_t  // First half of fall
        } else {
            2.0 * (1.0 - local_t) * (1.0 - local_t)  // Second half of fall
        }
    }
}
```

### Per-Character Phase Variation:
```rust
fn char_phase_offset(&self, char_idx: usize) -> f64 {
    // Deterministic pseudo-random based on seed and index
    let hash = self.seed.wrapping_mul(char_idx as u64 + 1);
    (hash % 1000) as f64 / 1000.0 * self.phase_variation
}

// In brightness calculation:
let t = (time * speed + char_phase_offset(idx)).rem_euclid(1.0);
let brightness = min_brightness + (1.0 - min_brightness) * breathing_curve(t, asymmetry);
```

This creates a subtle wave effect across the text as characters breathe slightly out of sync.

## Files
crates/ftui-extras/src/text_effects.rs

## Unit Tests (REQUIRED)
- test_organic_asymmetric_rise_fast: Rise duration < fall duration
- test_organic_symmetric_equal: asymmetry=0 has equal rise/fall
- test_organic_phase_variation_spreads: Adjacent chars different phase
- test_organic_seed_deterministic: Same seed = same pattern
- test_organic_min_brightness_floor: Never below min
- test_organic_max_brightness_ceiling: Peaks at 1.0
- test_organic_cycle_complete: Returns to start after 1/speed seconds

## Visual Tests
- Snapshot organic pulse at t=0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5 (shows asymmetry)
- Side-by-side: regular Pulse vs OrganicPulse

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Text Effects: Organic Breathing Pulse
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

 {"id":"bd-27v","title":"Implement Subscriptions and async command integration","description":"PHASE 6 - SUBSCRIPTIONS + ASYNC

## Background
Subscriptions provide continuous event sources (timers, file watchers, network). Async integration allows non-blocking operations without blocking the UI.

## Technical Requirements

### Subscription System

pub trait Subscription<M> {
    /// Unique ID for deduplication
    fn id(&self) -> SubId;
    
    /// Start producing messages
    fn start(&self, sender: mpsc::Sender<M>);
    
    /// Stop producing messages
    fn stop(&self);
}

pub type SubId = u64;

// Built-in subscriptions
pub mod subscriptions {
    /// Tick at fixed interval
    pub fn every<M: From<Tick>>(duration: Duration) -> impl Subscription<M>;
    
    /// File system watcher
    pub fn watch_file<M: From<FileEvent>>(path: PathBuf) -> impl Subscription<M>;
    
    /// Window resize events
    pub fn window_resize<M: From<Resize>>() -> impl Subscription<M>;
    
    /// Focus change events
    pub fn focus<M: From<Focus>>() -> impl Subscription<M>;
}

### Model Integration
pub trait Model: Sized {
    type Message;
    
    // Existing
    fn update(&mut self, msg: Self::Message) -> Cmd<Self::Message>;
    fn view(&self, frame: &mut Frame);
    
    // New: declare active subscriptions
    fn subscriptions(&self) -> Vec<Box<dyn Subscription<Self::Message>>> {
        vec![]  // Default: no subscriptions
    }
}

### Subscription Lifecycle
The runtime:
1. Calls model.subscriptions() after each update
2. Compares with previous subscriptions by id()
3. Starts new subscriptions
4. Stops removed subscriptions
5. Routes subscription messages to update()

### Async Commands (Feature: async)

#[cfg(feature = "async")]
pub enum Cmd<M> {
    // Existing...
    
    /// Execute async operation
    Async(BoxFuture<'static, M>),
    
    /// Execute blocking operation on threadpool
    Blocking(Box<dyn FnOnce() -> M + Send + 'static>),
}

#[cfg(feature = "async")]
impl<M: Send + 'static> Cmd<M> {
    pub fn async_fn<F, Fut>(f: F) -> Self
    where
        F: FnOnce() -> Fut + Send + 'static,
        Fut: Future<Output = M> + Send + 'static,
    {
        Cmd::Async(Box::pin(async move { f().await }))
    }
    
    pub fn blocking<F>(f: F) -> Self
    where
        F: FnOnce() -> M + Send + 'static,
    {
        Cmd::Blocking(Box::new(f))
    }
}

### Async Runtime Integration

#[cfg(feature = "tokio")]
pub struct TokioRuntime { ... }

#[cfg(feature = "async-std")]
pub struct AsyncStdRuntime { ... }

// Default: simple threadpool
pub struct ThreadPoolRuntime { ... }

### Deterministic Simulator Compatibility
Subscriptions must be mockable for testing:

pub struct MockSubscription<M> {
    id: SubId,
    events: Vec<M>,
}

impl<M> Subscription<M> for MockSubscription<M> {
    fn start(&self, sender: mpsc::Sender<M>) {
        for msg in &self.events {
            sender.send(msg.clone()).ok();
        }
    }
}

## Tests Required
- [ ] Subscription start/stop lifecycle
- [ ] Subscription deduplication by id
- [ ] Tick subscription fires correctly
- [ ] Async command executes
- [ ] Blocking command on threadpool
- [ ] Simulator with mock subscriptions
- [ ] Tokio integration (if feature enabled)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Subscription trait defined
- [ ] Built-in tick subscription
- [ ] Model.subscriptions() integration
- [ ] Async Cmd variant (feature-gated)
- [ ] Threadpool runtime (default)
- [ ] Tokio runtime (optional)
- [ ] Simulator compatibility
